Theorem 4.3 (Airy Stress Function). For plane problems without body forces, any stress field derived from an Airy function φ satisfying the biharmonic equation ∇⁴φ = 0 represents a valid elasticity solution.

4.6.3 4.6.3 Solution Methods

Common approaches for solving biharmonic equations include:

  1. Polynomial solutions: φ = Σₙ aₙx₁ᵐx₂ⁿ
  2. Complex variable methods: Using holomorphic functions
  3. Fourier series: For rectangular domains
  4. Finite element methods: For complex geometries

Example 4.2: Pure Bending of a Rectangular Beam

Consider a beam of height h and length L under pure bending moment M. The Airy function is: φ(x₁, x₂) = Ax₁x₂² + Bx₂⁴

where A and B are determined by boundary conditions. The stress field becomes: σ₁₁ = 2Ax₁ + 12Bx₂² σ₂₂ = 0
σ₁₂ = -Ax₂

For pure bending with M applied at the ends:

# Pure bending Airy function solution
function airy_pure_bending(M, I, x1, x2)
    # M: bending moment, I: second moment of area
    # x1: axial coordinate, x2: transverse coordinate
    A = -M / I
    B = 0
    
    # Stress components
    sigma11 = A * x2  # Linear stress distribution
    sigma22 = 0.0
    sigma12 = 0.0
    
    return sigma11, sigma22, sigma12
end

# Example: Steel beam
E = 200e9  # Pa
h = 0.1    # m (beam height)  
L = 1.0    # m (beam length)
I = h^3 / 12  # Second moment of area
M = 1000   # N⋅m

# Stress distribution across height
x2_range = -h/2:h/100:h/2
stresses = [airy_pure_bending(M, I, 0.5*L, x2)[1] for x2 in x2_range]

println("Maximum bending stress: $(maximum(abs.(stresses))/1e6) MPa")
# Should give σ_max = Mh/(2I) = 6M/h² for rectangular beam

using Plots
plot(stresses./1e6, x2_range.*1000, 
     xlabel="Stress (MPa)", ylabel="Height (mm)",
     title="Bending Stress Distribution", linewidth=2)

4.7 4.7 Saint-Venant’s Principle

Saint-Venant’s principle provides crucial insight into the localization of stress concentrations and the validity of simplified boundary conditions.

Theorem 4.4 (Saint-Venant’s Principle). If a system of forces acting on a small region of the boundary of an elastic body is replaced by a statically equivalent system, then the stress distribution changes significantly only in the immediate vicinity of the loaded region. At distances large compared to the dimensions of the loaded region, the stresses are essentially unchanged.

This principle justifies replacing complex boundary tractions with simpler equivalent systems, provided we are interested in the far-field response. The decay rate is typically exponential with a characteristic length proportional to the minimum dimension of the loaded region.

4.7.1 4.7.1 Mathematical Formulation

Consider two boundary value problems with identical geometry, material properties, and body forces, but different boundary tractions t₁ and t₂ on a region S₀ ⊂ ∂Ω. If these tractions are statically equivalent:

∫{S₀} t₁ dA = ∫{S₀} t₂ dA ∫{S₀} x × t₁ dA = ∫{S₀} x × t₂ dA

then the difference in stress fields σ₁ - σ₂ decays exponentially away from S₀.

4.7.2 4.7.2 Applications

Saint-Venant’s principle enables: - Simplified modeling of complex loading conditions - Justification of engineering beam and plate theories - Design of grips and fixtures in experimental testing - Understanding of stress concentration decay

4.8 4.8 Variational Formulation

Linear elasticity admits a natural variational formulation that forms the basis for computational methods.

4.8.1 4.8.1 Principle of Virtual Work

Definition 4.7 (Virtual Work Principle). A displacement field u satisfying kinematic boundary conditions is in equilibrium if and only if: δW_internal = δW_external

for all kinematically admissible virtual displacements δu.

The internal virtual work is: δW_internal = ∫_Ω σ : δε dV = ∫_Ω σᵢⱼ δεᵢⱼ dV

The external virtual work includes body forces and surface tractions: δW_external = ∫Ω b · δu dV + ∫{∂Ω_t} t · δu dA

4.8.2 4.8.2 Weak Formulation

For linear elastic materials, substituting σ = C : ε into the virtual work principle:

∫_Ω C : ε(u) : δε dV = ∫Ω b · δu dV + ∫{∂Ω_t} t · δu dA

In component form: ∫_Ω Cᵢⱼₖₗ εₖₗ(u) δεᵢⱼ dV = ∫Ω bᵢ δuᵢ dV + ∫{∂Ω_t} tᵢ δuᵢ dA

This weak formulation is equivalent to the strong form (Navier equations + boundary conditions) but requires only first derivatives of displacements, making it suitable for finite element discretization.

4.8.3 4.8.3 Energy Methods

The total potential energy functional is: Π(u) = ½∫_Ω σ : ε dV - ∫Ω b · u dV - ∫{∂Ω_t} t · u dA

The equilibrium displacement field minimizes this functional: δΠ = 0

This minimum principle forms the basis for Rayleigh-Ritz approximation methods and finite element formulations.

4.9 4.9 Finite Element Methods for Linear Elasticity

The finite element method provides the dominant computational approach for solving linear elasticity problems in complex geometries.

4.9.1 4.9.1 Discretization

The domain Ω is partitioned into elements Ωₑ with nodes at positions Xₐ. Displacements are interpolated using shape functions Nₐ(X):

uʰ(X) = Σₐ Nₐ(X) uₐ

where uₐ are nodal displacement vectors.

The strain field becomes: εʰ = Σₐ Bₐ uₐ

where Bₐ are strain-displacement matrices derived from shape function derivatives.

4.9.2 4.9.2 Element Matrices

The element stiffness matrix is: Kₑ = ∫_{Ωₑ} Bᵀ C B dV

The element force vector includes body forces and surface tractions: fₑ = ∫{Ωₑ} Nᵀ b dV + ∫{∂Ωₑ∩∂Ω_t} Nᵀ t dA

4.9.3 4.9.3 Global System

Assembly of element contributions yields the global system: K u = f

where K is the global stiffness matrix, u contains all nodal displacements, and f is the global force vector.

Example 4.3: Finite Element Analysis of Cantilever Beam

using Ferrite, LinearAlgebra

function cantilever_fem()
    # Create 2D cantilever mesh
    grid = generate_grid(Triangle, (20, 5), Vec{2}((0.0, 0.0)), Vec{2}((4.0, 1.0)))
    
    # Define interpolation and quadrature
    ip = Lagrange{2, RefTetrahedron, 1}()  # Linear triangular elements
    qr = QuadratureRule{2, RefTetrahedron}(2)  # Quadrature rule
    cv = CellVectorValues(qr, ip)
    
    # Define material properties (steel)
    E = 200e9  # Young's modulus (Pa)
    ν = 0.3    # Poisson's ratio
    
    # Plane stress stiffness matrix
    D = E / (1 - ν^2) * [1.0  ν    0.0;
                         ν    1.0  0.0;
                         0.0  0.0  (1-ν)/2]
    
    # Distributed load (pressure on top edge)
    p = 1000.0  # Pa
    
    println("Cantilever FE analysis:")
    println("Length: 4.0 m, Height: 1.0 m")
    println("Load: $p Pa on top edge")
    println("Material: E = $(E/1e9) GPa, ν = $ν")
    
    # Assembly would continue here with standard FE procedures...
    # This example demonstrates the setup for linear elasticity FE analysis
    
    return grid
end

grid = cantilever_fem()
println("Mesh created with $(getnnodes(grid)) nodes and $(getncells(grid)) elements")

4.10 4.10 Connections

Linear elasticity serves as the foundation for numerous extensions within continuum mechanics. The linearization framework introduced here extends naturally to:

  • Dynamic problems (Chapter 10): Adding inertial terms ρü to the Navier equations
  • Thermal effects: Incorporating thermal strains εₜₕ = α ΔT I in constitutive relations
  • Hyperelasticity (Chapter 5): Finite deformation generalizations using nonlinear strain measures
  • Viscoelasticity (Chapter 6): Time-dependent constitutive relations through hereditary integrals
  • Biological applications (Chapter 7): Specialization to soft tissue behavior with appropriate constitutive models

The variational formulation developed here provides the mathematical foundation for finite element methods applied throughout continuum mechanics, while the symmetry classification scheme extends to all material classes encountered in advanced applications.

The Airy stress function approach demonstrates how analytical solutions remain valuable for validation of computational methods and development of physical intuition, even in an era dominated by numerical simulation.

4.11 4.11 Exercises

  1. Constitutive Relations: Derive the inverse relations expressing the Lamé constants λ and μ in terms of Young’s modulus E and Poisson’s ratio ν. Show that the requirement of positive definite strain energy implies μ > 0 and -1 < ν < 0.5.

  2. Orthotropic Material: A wood specimen has elastic constants E₁ = 12 GPa, E₂ = 1.2 GPa, E₃ = 0.8 GPa, ν₁₂ = 0.4, ν₁₃ = 0.5, ν₂₃ = 0.3, G₁₂ = 0.7 GPa, G₁₃ = 0.8 GPa, G₂₃ = 0.06 GPa. Compute the full compliance matrix and verify the symmetry conditions.

  3. Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain: For the same loading and geometry, compare the maximum stress predictions from plane stress and plane strain analyses. Under what conditions do they agree closely?

  4. Navier Equations: Show that for isotropic materials under zero body forces, any displacement field of the form u = ∇φ + ∇ × ψ (where ∇ · ψ = 0) satisfies the Navier equations if ∇²φ = 0 and ∇²ψ = 0.

  5. Julia Implementation - Stress Transformation: Write a Julia function that transforms the stress tensor from one coordinate system to another using the transformation matrix Q. Verify your implementation using the principal stress calculation.

function stress_transform(sigma, Q)
    # sigma: 3x3 stress tensor in original coordinates
    # Q: 3x3 rotation matrix from original to new coordinates
    # Returns: transformed stress tensor
    
    # Your implementation here
end

# Test with known case

  1. Airy Function: Find the Airy stress function for a cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section under end loading P. Verify that the resulting stress distribution satisfies equilibrium and boundary conditions.

  2. Saint-Venant’s Principle: Using the Airy function approach, analyze the stress distribution near a concentrated load on the edge of a half-plane. Show how the stress field transitions from the local Saint-Venant region to the far-field solution.

  3. Julia Implementation - 2D Elasticity: Implement a finite difference solver for the biharmonic equation ∇⁴φ = 0 on a rectangular domain. Use your solver to analyze a plate with a circular hole under uniform tension.

function biharmonic_solver(nx, ny, boundary_conditions)
    # nx, ny: grid points in each direction  
    # boundary_conditions: struct containing BC information
\\newpage

# Chapter 5: Hyperelasticity

Hyperelastic materials form a fundamental class of nonlinear solid mechanics where the stress derives entirely from a strain energy function. Unlike the linear elastic materials of Chapter 4, hyperelastic materials can undergo large deformations while maintaining the conservative property that stress work depends only on the current configuration, not the deformation path. This chapter develops the mathematical framework of hyperelasticity and examines several classical models that capture the behavior of rubber-like materials and soft biological tissues.

## Strain Energy Functions and Thermodynamic Foundations

The defining characteristic of hyperelastic materials is the existence of a strain energy density function from which all stress measures can be derived. This function encodes the material's response to deformation in a thermodynamically consistent manner.

> **Definition 5.1 (Hyperelastic Material).** A material is hyperelastic if there exists a strain energy density function W such that the stress tensor can be derived as the gradient of W with respect to an appropriate strain measure.

The strain energy function can be expressed in terms of various kinematic measures from Chapter 1. The three most common formulations are:

1. **Lagrangian formulation**: W(E) where E is the Green strain tensor
2. **Principal invariant formulation**: W(I₁, I₂, I₃) using invariants of the right Cauchy-Green tensor C
3. **Deformation gradient formulation**: W(F) directly in terms of the deformation gradient

For the Lagrangian formulation, the second Piola-Kirchhoff stress derives from:

S = ∂W/∂E = 2∂W/∂C


since E = (C - I)/2. The first Piola-Kirchhoff stress follows as:

P = FS = F(∂W/∂E)


and the Cauchy stress becomes:

σ = J⁻¹PFᵀ = J⁻¹F(∂W/∂E)Fᵀ


where J = det(F) is the Jacobian of deformation.

For the principal invariant formulation, we express W in terms of the principal invariants:

I₁ = tr(C) = λ₁² + λ₂² + λ₃² I₂ = ½[(tr(C))² - tr(C²)] = λ₁²λ₂² + λ₂²λ₃² + λ₃²λ₁² I₃ = det(C) = λ₁²λ₂²λ₃² = J²


where λᵢ are the principal stretches. The second Piola-Kirchhoff stress then becomes:

S = 2(∂W/∂I₁ + I₁∂W/∂I₂)I - 2∂W/∂I₂C + 2I₃∂W/∂I₃C⁻¹


> **Theorem 5.1 (Stress-Energy Relations).** For a hyperelastic material with strain energy function W(C), the various stress measures satisfy:
> - S = 2∂W/∂C
> - P = 2F∂W/∂C  
> - σ = 2J⁻¹F(∂W/∂C)Fᵀ

*Proof.* These relations follow directly from the chain rule and the definitions of the stress tensors. For the second Piola-Kirchhoff stress, we use E = (C - I)/2, so ∂E/∂C = I/2, giving S = ∂W/∂E = (∂W/∂E)(∂E/∂C)⁻¹ = 2∂W/∂C. The remaining relations follow from the standard transformations between stress measures established in Chapter 2. □

## Neo-Hookean Model

The neo-Hookean model represents the simplest hyperelastic material that can describe large deformations. It extends Hooke's law to the finite strain regime while maintaining mathematical tractability.

> **Definition 5.2 (Neo-Hookean Material).** A neo-Hookean material has strain energy density:
> ```
> W(I₁, I₃) = μ/2(I₁ - 3) - μln(√I₃) + λ/2(ln(√I₃))²
> ```
> where μ and λ are the Lamé parameters related to the shear modulus and bulk modulus.

For incompressible materials (I₃ = J² = 1), the neo-Hookean energy reduces to:

W(I₁) = μ/2(I₁ - 3)


The corresponding second Piola-Kirchhoff stress for the compressible case is:

S = μI - μC⁻¹ + λ(ln J)C⁻¹


For the incompressible case:

S = μI - pC⁻¹


where p is a hydrostatic pressure determined from incompressibility constraints.

### Example 5.1: Uniaxial Extension of Neo-Hookean Material

Consider uniaxial extension of an incompressible neo-Hookean bar in the x₁ direction with stretch λ. The deformation gradient is:

F = diag(λ, λ⁻¹/², λ⁻¹/²)


The right Cauchy-Green tensor becomes:

C = F^T F = diag(λ², λ⁻¹, λ⁻¹)


The first invariant is:

I₁ = λ² + 2λ⁻¹


The strain energy per unit reference volume is:

W = μ/2(λ² + 2λ⁻¹ - 3)


The second Piola-Kirchhoff stress components are:

S₁₁ = μ - pλ⁻² S₂₂ = S₃₃ = μ - pλ


From the traction-free boundary conditions σ₂₂ = σ₃₃ = 0, we find:

p = μλ


The nominal stress in the loading direction becomes:

P₁₁ = S₁₁ = μ(1 - λ⁻³)


This gives the characteristic stiffening behavior under extension and the ability to sustain large compressive strains that distinguishes rubber-like materials from linear elastic solids.

## Mooney-Rivlin Model

The Mooney-Rivlin model extends the neo-Hookean model by including dependence on the second invariant I₂, providing better agreement with experimental data for rubber and soft biological tissues.

> **Definition 5.3 (Mooney-Rivlin Material).** A Mooney-Rivlin material has strain energy density:
> ```
> W(I₁, I₂) = C₁(I₁ - 3) + C₂(I₂ - 3)
> ```
> for the incompressible case, where C₁ and C₂ are material parameters.

The compressible Mooney-Rivlin model includes a volumetric term:

W(I₁, I₂, I₃) = C₁(I₁ - 3) + C₂(I₂ - 3) + f(J)


where f(J) is a function ensuring proper volumetric response, such as:

f(J) = κ/2(J - 1)²


with κ being the bulk modulus.

The second Piola-Kirchhoff stress for incompressible Mooney-Rivlin materials is:

S = 2C₁I + 2C₂(I₁I - C) - pC⁻¹


When C₂ = 0, the model reduces to the neo-Hookean case with μ = 2C₁.

### Example 5.2: Simple Shear of Mooney-Rivlin Material

Consider simple shear deformation with deformation gradient:

F = [1 γ 0] [0 1 0] [0 0 1]


where γ is the shear strain. The right Cauchy-Green tensor is:

C = [1+γ² γ 0] [γ 1 0] [0 0 1]


The invariants are:

I₁ = 3 + γ² I₂ = 3 + γ²
I₃ = 1


The strain energy density becomes:

W = C₁γ² + C₂γ²= (C₁ + C₂)γ²


The shear stress component σ₁₂ = γ(C₁ + C₂), giving an effective shear modulus of μ = C₁ + C₂, consistent with small strain linear elasticity.

## Ogden Model

The Ogden model provides exceptional flexibility in fitting experimental data by expressing the strain energy in terms of principal stretches rather than invariants.

> **Definition 5.4 (Ogden Material).** An Ogden material has strain energy density:
> ```
> W(λ₁, λ₂, λ₃) = Σᵢ₌₁ᴺ μᵢ/αᵢ(λ₁^αᵢ + λ₂^αᵢ + λ₃^αᵢ - 3)
> ```
> for the incompressible case, where μᵢ and αᵢ are material parameters, and λᵢ are the principal stretches.

For N = 1 and α₁ = 2, the Ogden model reduces to the neo-Hookean model. Higher-order terms allow fitting complex stress-strain curves observed in biological tissues and filled elastomers.

The principal Cauchy stresses are:

σᵢ = λᵢ∂W/∂λᵢ - p = Σⱼ₌₁ᴺ μⱼλᵢ^αⱼ - p


The material parameters can be determined from experimental data using nonlinear regression techniques.

```julia
using Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL, ForwardDiff

# Ogden model implementation
function ogden_energy(λ, params)
    """
    Compute Ogden strain energy for principal stretches λ = [λ₁, λ₂, λ₃]
    params = [μ₁, α₁, μ₂, α₂, ..., μₙ, αₙ]
    """
    W = 0.0
    N = length(params) ÷ 2
    
    for i in 1:N
        μᵢ = params[2i-1]
        αᵢ = params[2i]
        W += μᵢ/αᵢ * (sum(λ .^ αᵢ) - 3.0)
    end
    
    return W
end

function ogden_stress(λ, params)
    """Compute principal Cauchy stresses"""
    N = length(params) ÷ 2
    σ = zeros(3)
    
    for i in 1:3
        for j in 1:N
            μⱼ = params[2j-1]
            αⱼ = params[2j]
            σ[i] += μⱼ * λ[i]^αⱼ
        end
    end
    
    # Subtract hydrostatic pressure for incompressible case
    p = sum(σ) / 3
    σ .- p
end

# Example: Fit Ogden model to uniaxial tension data
function fit_ogden_model(stretch_data, stress_data)
    """Fit 2-term Ogden model to experimental data"""
    
    function objective(params, data)
        stretch_exp, stress_exp = data
        error = 0.0
        
        for (λ, σ_exp) in zip(stretch_exp, stress_exp)
            # Uniaxial tension: λ₁ = λ, λ₂ = λ₃ = λ^(-1/2) for incompressible
            λ_vec = [λ, λ^(-0.5), λ^(-0.5)]
            σ_model = ogden_stress(λ_vec, params)
            error += (σ_model[1] - σ_exp)^2
        end
        
        return error
    end
    
    # Initial guess: 2-term model
    p0 = [1.0, 2.0, 0.1, -2.0]  # [μ₁, α₁, μ₂, α₂]
    
    prob = OptimizationProblem(objective, p0, (stretch_data, stress_data))
    sol = solve(prob, BFGS())
    
    return sol.u
end

4.12 Arruda-Boyce (Eight-Chain) Model

The Arruda-Boyce model derives from statistical mechanics considerations of polymer chain networks, providing a physically motivated approach to hyperelasticity.

Definition 5.5 (Arruda-Boyce Material). The Arruda-Boyce model has strain energy density:

W = μN[β/tanh(β) - ln(sinh(β)/β)]

where β is the solution to β/tanh(β) = λ_chain/√N, with λ_chain = √(I₁/3) being the effective chain stretch and N the number of rigid links per chain.

For moderate deformations, the Arruda-Boyce model can be approximated by the series expansion:

W = μ/2[(I₁ - 3) + 1/(20N)(I₁² - 9) + 11/(1050N²)(I₁³ - 27) + ...]

This model naturally incorporates strain stiffening at large deformations due to the finite extensibility of polymer chains, making it particularly suitable for modeling biological tissues and elastomeric materials.

4.12.1 Example 5.3: Implementation of Arruda-Boyce Model

using NLsolve

function arruda_boyce_energy(I1, μ, N)
    """
    Compute Arruda-Boyce strain energy
    I1: first invariant of C
    μ: shear modulus
    N: number of chain links
    """
    
    # Effective chain stretch
    λ_chain = sqrt(I1 / 3)
    
    # Solve for β: β/tanh(β) = λ_chain/√N
    function β_equation(β)
        return β / tanh(β) - λ_chain / sqrt(N)
    end
    
    # Initial guess for β
    β_guess = λ_chain / sqrt(N)
    β_sol = nlsolve(β_equation, [β_guess])
    β = β_sol.zero[1]
    
    # Strain energy
    W = μ * N * (β / tanh(β) - log(sinh(β) / β))
    
    return W
end

function arruda_boyce_stress_I1(I1, μ, N)
    """Compute ∂W/∂I1 for stress calculation"""
    
    # Use automatic differentiation for derivative
    dW_dI1 = ForwardDiff.derivative(I1 -> arruda_boyce_energy(I1, μ, N), I1)
    
    return dW_dI1
end

# Approximate form for moderate deformations
function arruda_boyce_approx(I1, μ, N, terms=3)
    """Series approximation of Arruda-Boyce model"""
    
    W = 0.0
    
    # Leading term (neo-Hookean)
    W += μ/2 * (I1 - 3)
    
    if terms >= 2
        # Second-order correction
        W += μ/(20*N) * (I1^2 - 9)
    end
    
    if terms >= 3
        # Third-order correction  
        W += 11*μ/(1050*N^2) * (I1^3 - 27)
    end
    
    return W
end

4.13 Nearly Incompressible Formulations

Many soft materials, including biological tissues and elastomers, exhibit nearly incompressible behavior with Poisson ratios approaching 0.5. Standard displacement-based finite element formulations suffer from volumetric locking in this limit, requiring special treatment.

4.13.1 Penalty Method

The penalty method enforces near-incompressibility by adding a large penalty term to the strain energy:

W_total = W_deviatoric + κ/2(J - 1)²

where κ is a penalty parameter much larger than the deviatoric material moduli. The volumetric part of the deformation is penalized, driving J toward unity.

4.13.2 Mixed Formulations

Mixed formulations treat the hydrostatic pressure as an independent field variable, avoiding volumetric locking while maintaining exact incompressibility in the limit.

Definition 5.6 (Mixed Hyperelastic Formulation). The mixed formulation introduces pressure p as an independent variable with strain energy:

W(F, p) = W_deviatoric(F̄) + U(J) + p(J - 1)

where F̄ = J^(-1/3)F is the isochoric part of the deformation gradient and U(J) provides regularization.

The corresponding stress measures become:

P = ∂W_deviatoric/∂F̄ : ∂F̄/∂F + pJF^(-T)

where the pressure p is determined from the constraint J = 1 (or nearly so).

function mixed_hyperelastic_residual!(R, state, params)
    """
    Compute residual for mixed hyperelastic formulation
    state = [u; p] where u are displacements, p are pressures
    """
    
    mesh, material = params
    ndofs_u = length(state) - mesh.nnodes  # displacement DOFs
    
    u = @view state[1:ndofs_u]
    p = @view state[ndofs_u+1:end]
    
    R_u = @view R[1:ndofs_u]        # displacement residual
    R_p = @view R[ndofs_u+1:end]    # pressure residual
    
    fill!(R, 0.0)
    
    # Loop over elements
    for elem in mesh.elements
        # Compute element deformation gradient F
        F = compute_deformation_gradient(elem, u)
        J = det(F)
        
        # Isochoric deformation gradient
        F_bar = J^(-1/3) * F
        
        # Deviatoric stress
        P_dev = deviatoric_stress(F_bar, material)
        
        # Total first Piola-Kirchhoff stress
        p_elem = interpolate_pressure(elem, p)
        P_total = P_dev + p_elem * J * inv(F)'
        
        # Assemble displacement residual
        assemble_displacement_residual!(R_u, elem, P_total)
        
        # Assemble pressure residual (incompressibility constraint)
        assemble_pressure_residual!(R_p, elem, J - 1.0)
    end
end

4.14 Polyconvexity and Existence of Minimizers

The mathematical well-posedness of hyperelastic boundary value problems depends critically on the convexity properties of the strain energy function. Polyconvexity provides sufficient conditions for the existence of minimizing deformations.

Definition 5.7 (Polyconvex Function). A function W(F) is polyconvex if it can be expressed as a convex function of F, Cof(F), and det(F), where Cof(F) is the cofactor matrix of F.

Theorem 5.2 (Ball’s Existence Theorem). Let Ω ⊂ ℝ³ be a bounded domain with Lipschitz boundary. If the strain energy density W(F) satisfies: 1. W is polyconvex 2. W(F) ≥ c(|F|^p + |Cof(F)|^q + |det(F)|^r) for some c > 0, p ≥ 3, q ≥ 3/2, r > 1 3. W(F) → ∞ as det(F) → 0⁺

Then the variational problem inf{∫_Ω W(∇φ)dx : φ ∈ A} has a solution, where A is the space of admissible deformations.

Proof. The proof relies on the direct method in the calculus of variations, using the polyconvexity to establish weak lower semicontinuity of the energy functional and the growth conditions to ensure coercivity. Details can be found in Ball (1977). □

Classical models exhibit varying degrees of polyconvexity:

  • Neo-Hookean: Polyconvex for compressible case
  • Mooney-Rivlin: Not generally polyconvex
  • Ogden: Polyconvex under certain parameter restrictions
function check_polyconvexity_ogden(μ_vec, α_vec)
    """
    Check necessary conditions for Ogden model polyconvexity
    Returns true if parameters satisfy Ball's conditions
    """
    
    N = length(μ_vec)
    
    # Condition 1: Σᵢ μᵢ ≥ 0
    if sum(μ_vec) < 0
        return false
    end
    
    # Condition 2: For each αᵢ ∈ (0,2), we need μᵢ ≥ 0
    for i in 1:N
        if 0 < α_vec[i] < 2 && μ_vec[i] < 0
            return false
        end
    end
    
    # Condition 3: For αᵢ ≥ 2, Ball's theorem provides sufficient conditions
    # (More complex analysis required for complete verification)
    
    return true  # Preliminary check passed
end

4.15 Material Stability and Strong Ellipticity

Material stability ensures that the material response is physically reasonable under arbitrary deformation modes. Strong ellipticity provides the mathematical condition for stability.

Definition 5.8 (Strong Ellipticity). A hyperelastic material satisfies strong ellipticity if for all unit vectors n and m:

C_{ijkl}n_i m_j n_k m_l > 0

where C_{ijkl} are the components of the elasticity tensor.

For hyperelastic materials, the elasticity tensor derives from the second derivatives of the strain energy:

C_{ijkl} = ∂²W/∂F_{ij}∂F_{kl}

Strong ellipticity ensures that the material cannot exhibit unstable behavior such as shear band formation or loss of uniqueness in boundary value problems.

4.15.1 Example 5.4: Strong Ellipticity for Neo-Hookean Materials

For the compressible neo-Hookean model, the strong ellipticity condition requires:

μ > 0  and  λ + 2μ/3 > 0

These conditions ensure positive shear modulus and bulk modulus, preventing material instabilities.

function strong_ellipticity_acoustic_tensor(F, material_params, n)
    """
    Compute acoustic tensor Q = C[n ⊗ n] for strong ellipticity check
    F: deformation gradient (3×3)  
    material_params: material parameters
    n: unit normal vector (3×1)
    """
    
    # Compute elasticity tensor (4th order, stored as 6×6 matrix)
    C = compute_elasticity_tensor(F, material_params)
    
    # Form acoustic tensor Q_ik = C_ijkl n_j n_l
    Q = zeros(3, 3)
    
    for i in 1:3
        for k in 1:3
            for j in 1:3
                for l in 1:3
                    Q[i,k] += C[i,j,k,l] * n[j] * n[l]
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return Q
end

function check_strong_ellipticity(F, material_params, num_directions=100)
    """
    Check strong ellipticity by sampling directions on unit sphere
    Returns true if Q is positive definite for all sampled directions
    """
    
    using LinearAlgebra
    
    for _ in 1:num_directions
        # Random unit vector
        n = randn(3)
        n = n / norm(n)
        
        # Compute acoustic tensor
        Q = strong_ellipticity_acoustic_tensor(F, material_params, n)
        
        # Check positive definiteness
        eigenvals = eigvals(Symmetric(Q))
        
        if minimum(eigenvals) ≤ 1e-12  # Numerical tolerance
            return false
        end
    end
    
    return true
end

4.16 Implementation in Finite Element Method

The implementation of hyperelastic materials in finite element codes requires careful attention to the nonlinear nature of the governing equations and the proper linearization for Newton-Raphson iterations.

The weak form of the equilibrium equations is:

∫_Ω P : ∇η dX = ∫_∂Ω T̄ · η dS

where η is a test function and T̄ are prescribed tractions.

The tangent stiffness matrix for Newton iterations requires the fourth-order elasticity tensor:

K_{ij} = ∫_Ω C_{ijkl} ∂η_i/∂X_k ∂u_l/∂X_j dX

4.16.1 Example 5.5: Finite Element Implementation

using SparseArrays, LinearAlgebra

struct HyperelasticElement
    nodes::Vector{Int}
    material::HyperelasticMaterial
    quadrature::GaussQuadrature
end

abstract type HyperelasticMaterial end

struct NeoHookean <: HyperelasticMaterial
    μ::Float64
    λ::Float64
end

function strain_energy_density(mat::NeoHookean, F)
    """Compute neo-Hookean strain energy density"""
    C = F' * F
    I1 = tr(C)
    J = det(F)
    
    return mat.μ/2 * (I1 - 3) - mat.μ * log(J) + mat.λ/2 * (log(J))^2
end

function first_piola_kirchhoff(mat::NeoHookean, F)
    """Compute first Piola-Kirchhoff stress"""
    J = det(F)
    F_inv_T = inv(F)'
    
    return mat.μ * F - mat.μ * F_inv_T + mat.λ * log(J) * F_inv_T
end

function elasticity_tensor(mat::NeoHookean, F)
    """Compute material tangent (elasticity tensor)"""
    J = det(F)
    F_inv = inv(F)
    F_inv_T = F_inv'
    
    # Fourth-order tensor stored as matrix for efficiency
    C = zeros(9, 9)  # Vectorized form of 3×3×3×3 tensor
    
    # Material tangent contributions
    # Simplified implementation - full tensor assembly requires careful indexing
    for i in 1:3, j in 1:3, k in 1:3, l in 1:3
        # Convert to matrix indices
        I = 3*(i-1) + j  
        J = 3*(k-1) + l
        
        # Neo-Hookean material tangent components
        δik_δjl = (i == k) * (j == l)
        C[I,J] = mat.μ * δik_δjl + 
                (mat.λ * log(J) - mat.μ) * F_inv_T[i,j] * F_inv_T[k,l] +
                mat.λ * F_inv_T[i,l] * F_inv_T[k,j]
    end
    
    return C
end

function element_residual_and_tangent!(R, K, elem::HyperelasticElement, u)
    """
    Compute element residual and tangent stiffness matrix
    """
    
    fill!(R, 0.0)
    fill!(K, 0.0)
    
    # Loop over quadrature points
    for qp in elem.quadrature.points
        # Shape function derivatives
        dN_dξ = shape_function_derivatives(qp.ξ)
        
        # Jacobian of reference element
        J0, dN_dX = reference_jacobian(elem.nodes, dN_dξ)
        
        # Deformation gradient
        F = deformation_gradient(dN_dX, u)
        
        # First Piola-Kirchhoff stress
        P = first_piola_kirchhoff(elem.material, F)
        
        # Material tangent
        C = elasticity_tensor(elem.material, F)
        
        # Quadrature weight
        w = qp.weight * J0
        
        # Assemble residual: R += ∫ P : ∇N dX
        for a in 1:length(elem.nodes)
            for i in 1:3
                for j in 1:3
                    R[3*(a-1) + i] += P[i,j] * dN_dX[a,j] * w
                end
            end
        end
        
        # Assemble tangent: K += ∫ C[∇N ⊗ ∇N] dX  
        for a in 1:length(elem.nodes), b in 1:length(elem.nodes)
            for i in 1:3, k in 1:3
                row = 3*(a-1) + i
                
                for j in 1:3, l in 1:3
                    col = 3*(b-1) + k
                    
                    # Material contribution
                    I = 3*(i-1) + j
                    J = 3*(k-1) + l
                    K[row, col] += C[I,J] * dN_dX[a,j] * dN_dX[b,l] * w
                    
                    # Geometric contribution (initial stress)
                    if j == l
                        K[row, col] += P[i,k] * dN_dX[a,j] * dN_dX[b,l] * w
                    end
                end
            end
        end
    end
end

4.17 Exercises

Exercise 5.1 (Theoretical). Prove that for any hyperelastic material, the stress power can be expressed as Ṗ : Ḟ = ∂W/∂F : Ḟ, where Ṗ and Ḟ are material time derivatives. Show that this implies energy conservation for adiabatic processes.

Exercise 5.2 (Computational). Implement the Mooney-Rivlin model in Julia and compute the stress response under equibiaxial extension (λ₁ = λ₂ = λ, λ₃ = λ⁻²). Plot the stress-stretch curves for different values of C₁ and C₂ and compare with the neo-Hookean limit.

Exercise 5.3 (Theoretical). Show that the Ogden model with parameters (μ₁ = μ, α₁ = 2) and (μ₂ = -μ/2, α₂ = -2) is equivalent to the neo-Hookean model for incompressible materials.

Exercise 5.4 (Computational). Write a Julia function to determine the Ogden model parameters that best fit experimental stress-strain data. Use the function to fit a 2- \newpage

5 Chapter 6: Viscoelasticity

Viscoelastic materials exhibit both elastic and viscous characteristics when undergoing deformation. Unlike pure elastic materials that respond instantaneously to applied loads, viscoelastic materials display time-dependent behavior: their stress-strain relationships depend on the history of loading. This behavior is ubiquitous in biological tissues, polymers, and many engineering materials, making viscoelasticity essential for understanding the mechanical response of soft matter.

The mathematical framework of viscoelasticity provides tools to model materials whose mechanical properties lie between those of ideal Hookean solids and Newtonian viscous fluids. This chapter develops the theory from linear viscoelasticity through finite deformation models, emphasizing both the mathematical structure and computational implementation of these theories.

5.1 Linear Viscoelasticity

5.1.1 Phenomenological Observations

Linear viscoelasticity manifests through several characteristic phenomena. Under constant stress (creep loading), a viscoelastic material continues to deform over time, approaching an asymptotic strain. Conversely, under constant strain (relaxation), the stress required to maintain that deformation decreases with time. These behaviors contrast sharply with elastic materials, where stress and strain maintain constant proportional relationships.

The mathematical description of linear viscoelasticity rests on the assumption that the material response remains linear in the stress and strain measures, even though the relationship involves time dependence and memory effects.

5.1.2 Creep and Relaxation Functions

Definition 6.1 (Creep Function). The creep function J(t) describes the strain response to a unit step stress applied at time t = 0:

ε(t) = J(t)σ₀H(t)

where σ₀ is the magnitude of the applied stress and H(t) is the Heaviside step function.

Definition 6.2 (Relaxation Function). The relaxation function G(t) describes the stress response required to maintain a unit step strain applied at time t = 0:

σ(t) = G(t)ε₀H(t)

where ε₀ is the magnitude of the applied strain.

For most materials, the creep function is monotonically increasing (J’(t) ≥ 0) while the relaxation function is monotonically decreasing (G’(t) ≤ 0). These functions are not independent; they satisfy an important reciprocal relationship.

Theorem 6.1 (Creep-Relaxation Reciprocity). The creep and relaxation functions are related through the convolution equation:

∫₀ᵗ G(t-τ) dJ(τ)/dτ dτ = H(t)

where the integral is understood in the Stieltjes sense.

Proof. Consider the response to a unit step strain followed by immediate removal. The stress during application is G(t), and the requirement that stress vanish after removal leads to the reciprocal relationship through Laplace transform methods. □

5.1.3 Boltzmann Superposition Principle

The fundamental principle governing linear viscoelastic behavior is the Boltzmann superposition principle, which states that the response to a combination of loading histories equals the sum of responses to individual histories.

Theorem 6.2 (Boltzmann Superposition). For a linear viscoelastic material, the stress at time t due to an arbitrary strain history ε(τ) for τ ∈ [0,t] is given by:

σ(t) = ∫₀ᵗ G(t-τ) dε(τ)/dτ dτ

Similarly, the strain response to an arbitrary stress history σ(τ) is:

ε(t) = ∫₀ᵗ J(t-τ) dσ(τ)/dτ dτ

This hereditary integral formulation captures the essential feature of viscoelastic materials: their response depends on the entire loading history, not merely the current state.

using Plots, QuadGK

# Define a simple relaxation function (exponential decay)
G(t, G₀=1.0, τ=2.0) = G₀ * exp(-t/τ)

# Define creep function (approximately reciprocal)
J(t, J₀=1.0, τ=2.0) = J₀ * (1 - exp(-t/τ))

# Boltzmann superposition for arbitrary strain history
function viscoelastic_stress(t_eval, strain_history, time_points, relaxation_func)
    stress = zeros(length(t_eval))
    
    for (i, t) in enumerate(t_eval)
        if t <= 0
            stress[i] = 0.0
            continue
        end
        
        # Numerical integration of hereditary integral
        integrand(τ) = begin
            # Linear interpolation of strain history
            if τ <= 0
                return 0.0
            elseif τ >= time_points[end]
                return 0.0
            else
                # Find strain rate by finite difference
                idx = searchsortedfirst(time_points, τ)
                if idx == 1
                    dε_dt = (strain_history[2] - strain_history[1]) / 
                           (time_points[2] - time_points[1])
                elseif idx > length(time_points)
                    dε_dt = 0.0
                else
                    dε_dt = (strain_history[idx] - strain_history[idx-1]) / 
                           (time_points[idx] - time_points[idx-1])
                end
                return relaxation_func(t - τ) * dε_dt
            end
        end
        
        # Integrate from 0 to t
        result, _ = quadgk(integrand, 0.0, t)
        stress[i] = result
    end
    
    return stress
end

# Example: Step strain followed by ramp unloading
t_points = 0:0.1:10
strain_hist = [t < 2 ? 0.1 : max(0, 0.1 - 0.02*(t-2)) for t in t_points]

stress_response = viscoelastic_stress(t_points, strain_hist, t_points, G)

# Plot results
p1 = plot(t_points, strain_hist, label="Applied Strain", 
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="Strain", lw=2)
p2 = plot(t_points, stress_response, label="Stress Response", 
          xlabel="Time", ylabel="Stress", lw=2, color=:red)
plot(p1, p2, layout=(2,1), size=(800,600))

5.2 Spring-Dashpot Models

5.2.1 Maxwell Model

The Maxwell model represents the simplest viscoelastic idealization, consisting of a spring and dashpot in series. This configuration captures stress relaxation behavior but fails to model creep accurately.

Definition 6.3 (Maxwell Model). The Maxwell model relates stress σ and strain ε through:

dσ/dt + σ/τ = E dε/dt

where E is the elastic modulus and τ = η/E is the relaxation time with η being the viscosity.

For the Maxwell model, the relaxation function takes the exponential form:

G(t) = E exp(-t/τ)

while the creep function grows linearly with time after an initial elastic response:

J(t) = 1/E + t/η

5.2.2 Kelvin-Voigt Model

The Kelvin-Voigt model places the spring and dashpot in parallel, providing excellent creep modeling but instantaneous stress response that is physically unrealistic for many applications.

Definition 6.4 (Kelvin-Voigt Model). The Kelvin-Voigt model satisfies:

σ = E ε + η dε/dt

The creep function for this model is:

J(t) = (1/E)(1 - exp(-t/τ))

where τ = η/E is the retardation time.

5.2.3 Standard Linear Solid

The standard linear solid (SLS) combines features of both Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt models, providing a more realistic description of viscoelastic behavior.

Definition 6.5 (Standard Linear Solid). The SLS model has the differential equation:

σ + p₁ dσ/dt = q₀ ε + q₁ dε/dt

where the parameters satisfy p₁ > 0 and q₁ > q₀p₁ for physical realizability.

Example 6.1 (SLS Parameter Identification). Consider experimental data showing a relaxation modulus that decays from G₀ = 10⁶ Pa to G∞ = 2×10⁵ Pa with a characteristic time of 100 seconds. The SLS parameters are:

  • q₀ = G∞ = 2×10⁵ Pa
  • q₁ = G₀ × τ = 10⁶ × 100 = 10⁸ Pa·s
  • p₁ = τ = 100 s

The relaxation function becomes: G(t) = G∞ + (G₀ - G∞)exp(-t/τ)

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Standard Linear Solid implementation
struct SLSModel
    E₀::Float64    # Instantaneous modulus
    E∞::Float64    # Long-term modulus  
    τ::Float64     # Relaxation time
end

# Relaxation function for SLS
relaxation_modulus(model::SLSModel, t) = 
    model.E∞ + (model.E₀ - model.E∞) * exp(-t/model.τ)

# Creep function for SLS (analytical solution)
function creep_compliance(model::SLSModel, t)
    E₀, E∞, τ = model.E₀, model.E∞, model.τ
    J∞ = 1/E∞
    J₀ = 1/E₀
    return J∞ - (J∞ - J₀) * exp(-t * E∞/(τ * E₀))
end

# Create SLS model from Example 6.1
sls = SLSModel(1e6, 2e5, 100.0)

# Plot relaxation and creep functions
t_range = 0:1:500
G_vals = [relaxation_modulus(sls, t) for t in t_range]
J_vals = [creep_compliance(sls, t) for t in t_range]

p1 = plot(t_range, G_vals./1e6, label="G(t)", xlabel="Time (s)", 
          ylabel="Relaxation Modulus (MPa)", lw=2)
p2 = plot(t_range, J_vals.*1e6, label="J(t)", xlabel="Time (s)", 
          ylabel="Creep Compliance (1/MPa)", lw=2)
plot(p1, p2, layout=(1,2), size=(1000,400))

5.2.4 Generalized Maxwell Model and Prony Series

Real materials often exhibit multiple relaxation times, requiring more sophisticated models than the simple three-parameter SLS. The generalized Maxwell model uses multiple Maxwell elements in parallel.

Definition 6.6 (Generalized Maxwell Model). The relaxation modulus takes the Prony series form:

G(t) = G∞ + ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Gᵢ exp(-t/τᵢ)

where G∞ is the long-term modulus, Gᵢ are the relaxation strengths, and τᵢ are the relaxation times.

Example 6.2 (Prony Series Fitting). Dynamic mechanical analysis of a polymer shows relaxation behavior requiring three exponential terms. Using experimental data, we fit:

G(t) = 2×10⁵ + 3×10⁵ exp(-t/10) + 4×10⁵ exp(-t/100) + 1×10⁵ exp(-t/1000)

using LsqFit, Random

# Generate synthetic relaxation data with noise
function generate_relaxation_data(t_exp, G_inf, g_coeffs, tau_values)
    G_true = G_inf .+ sum([g_coeffs[i] * exp.(-t_exp./tau_values[i]) 
                          for i in 1:length(g_coeffs)])
    # Add 5% noise
    Random.seed!(42)
    noise = 0.05 * G_true .* randn(length(t_exp))
    return G_true + noise
end

# Prony series model for fitting
function prony_model(t, p)
    G_inf = p[1]
    n_terms = (length(p) - 1) ÷ 2
    G_vals = fill(G_inf, length(t))
    
    for i in 1:n_terms
        g_i = p[2*i]
        tau_i = p[2*i + 1]
        G_vals += g_i * exp.(-t./tau_i)
    end
    return G_vals
end

# Experimental time points (log scale for wide range)
t_exp = 10.0.^(-1:0.1:4)

# True parameters for synthetic data
G_inf_true = 2e5
g_true = [3e5, 4e5, 1e5]
tau_true = [10.0, 100.0, 1000.0]

# Generate synthetic data
G_data = generate_relaxation_data(t_exp, G_inf_true, g_true, tau_true)

# Initial parameter guess
p0 = [1.5e5, 2e5, 15.0, 5e5, 80.0, 8e4, 1200.0]

# Fit Prony series
fit_result = curve_fit(prony_model, t_exp, G_data, p0)
p_fitted = fit_result.param

println("Fitted parameters:")
println("G∞ = $(p_fitted[1]/1e5:.2f) × 10⁵ Pa")
for i in 1:3
    g_fit = p_fitted[2*i]/1e5
    tau_fit = p_fitted[2*i + 1]
    println("G$i = $(g_fit:.2f) × 10⁵ Pa, τ$i = $(tau_fit:.1f) s")
end

# Plot fit quality
G_fitted = prony_model(t_exp, p_fitted)
plot(t_exp, G_data./1e5, seriestype=:scatter, label="Data", 
     xscale=:log10, xlabel="Time (s)", ylabel="G(t) (10⁵ Pa)")
plot!(t_exp, G_fitted./1e5, label="Prony Fit", lw=2)

5.3 Complex Modulus and Dynamic Testing

5.3.1 Frequency Domain Analysis

Dynamic mechanical testing subjects materials to sinusoidal loading, revealing frequency-dependent properties through the complex modulus formulation.

Definition 6.7 (Complex Modulus). For harmonic loading ε(t) = ε₀e^(iωt), the complex modulus is:

G*(ω) = G’(ω) + iG’’(ω)

where G’(ω) is the storage modulus and G’’(ω) is the loss modulus.

The storage modulus represents the elastic energy storage capability, while the loss modulus quantifies energy dissipation. Their ratio defines the loss tangent:

tan δ = G’‘(ω)/G’(ω)

5.3.2 Correspondence Principle

The correspondence principle provides a powerful method for solving viscoelastic boundary value problems by leveraging solutions from elasticity theory.

Theorem 6.3 (Correspondence Principle). Linear viscoelastic problems can be solved by: 1. Solving the corresponding elastic problem in the Laplace domain 2. Replacing elastic constants with their Laplace transforms 3. Inverting the transform to obtain the time-domain solution

Example 6.3 (Viscoelastic Cantilever Beam). Consider a cantilever beam under suddenly applied tip load P. The elastic deflection is:

δₑ(x) = (P L³/3EI)[3x/L - (x/L)³]

Using the correspondence principle with G(t) = E exp(-t/τ), the viscoelastic deflection becomes:

δ(x,t) = (P L³/3I)[3x/L - (x/L)³] ∫₀ᵗ J(t-τ) δ(τ) dτ

where J(t) = (1/E)[1 + (t/τ)] for the Maxwell model.

using FFTW, DSP

# Correspondence principle implementation for Maxwell model
function maxwell_response_laplace(s, G₀, τ)
    """Relaxation modulus in Laplace domain"""
    return G₀ * τ / (1 + s*τ)
end

function solve_viscoelastic_cantilever(L, I, P, material_params, x_eval, t_eval)
    """
    Solve cantilever beam deflection using correspondence principle
    """
    G₀, τ = material_params
    
    # Elastic solution at tip (x = L)
    δ_elastic_tip = P * L^3 / (3 * G₀ * I)
    
    # Time-domain solution using inverse Laplace transform approximation
    deflections = zeros(length(x_eval), length(t_eval))
    
    for (i, x) in enumerate(x_eval)
        # Elastic deflection at position x
        ξ = x/L
        shape_factor = 3*ξ - ξ^3
        δ_elastic_x = δ_elastic_tip * shape_factor
        
        # Viscoelastic response (Maxwell model analytical solution)
        for (j, t) in enumerate(t_eval)
            if t <= 0
                deflections[i, j] = 0.0
            else
                # For step load and Maxwell model
                deflections[i, j] = δ_elastic_x * (1 + t/τ)
            end
        end
    end
    
    return deflections
end

# Beam parameters
L = 1.0  # Length (m)
I = 1e-6  # Second moment of area (m⁴)
P = 100.0  # Applied load (N)
G₀ = 1e9   # Initial modulus (Pa)
τ = 300.0   # Relaxation time (s)

# Evaluation points
x_points = [0.0, 0.25*L, 0.5*L, 0.75*L, L]
t_points = 0:50:1000

# Solve viscoelastic response
deflections = solve_viscoelastic_cantilever(L, I, P, (G₀, τ), x_points, t_points)

# Plot deflection evolution at beam tip
plot(t_points, deflections[end, :] * 1000, 
     xlabel="Time (s)", ylabel="Tip Deflection (mm)",
     label="Viscoelastic Response", lw=2)

5.4 Nonlinear Viscoelasticity

5.4.1 Quasi-Linear Viscoelasticity

Many biological tissues exhibit nonlinear stress-strain relationships combined with linear viscoelastic time dependence. The quasi-linear viscoelastic (QLV) theory, developed by Fung, addresses this behavior.

Definition 6.8 (Quasi-Linear Viscoelasticity). The QLV constitutive law is:

σ(t) = ∫₋∞ᵗ G(t-τ) ∂σᵉ(ε(τ))/∂ε dε(τ)/dτ dτ

where σᵉ(ε) is the elastic response function and G(t) is the reduced relaxation function.

This formulation assumes that the nonlinearity and time dependence separate: the instantaneous nonlinear response σᵉ(ε) is modulated by the linear viscoelastic relaxation G(t).

Example 6.4 (QLV Model for Arterial Tissue). Arterial walls often follow an exponential elastic response:

σᵉ(ε) = A(e^(Bε) - 1)

Combined with exponential relaxation G(t) = exp(-t/τ), this gives the QLV response for step strain ε₀:

σ(t) = AB e^(Bε₀) exp(-t/τ)

5.4.2 Finite Deformation Viscoelasticity

For large deformations, the kinematics become nonlinear, requiring careful consideration of stress and strain measures. The multiplicative decomposition approach proves most successful.

Definition 6.9 (Multiplicative Decomposition). The deformation gradient F decomposes as:

F = F^e F^v

where F^v represents viscous flow and F^e represents elastic distortion from the intermediate configuration.

This decomposition leads to the evolution equation:

∂F^v/∂t = L^v F^v

where L^v is the viscous velocity gradient in the intermediate configuration.

using LinearAlgebra, ForwardDiff

# Finite deformation viscoelasticity implementation
struct FiniteViscoelasticMaterial
    μ::Float64      # Shear modulus
    λ::Float64      # Lame parameter  
    η::Float64      # Viscosity
    K::Float64      # Bulk modulus
end

function neo_hookean_stress(F, μ, K)
    """Neo-Hookean hyperelastic stress"""
    J = det(F)
    C = F' * F  # Right Cauchy-Green tensor
    b = F * F'  # Left Cauchy-Green tensor
    
    # Second Piola-Kirchhoff stress
    I₁ = tr(C)
    S = μ * (I - inv(C)) + (K * log(J) / J) * inv(C)
    
    # Convert to Cauchy stress
    σ = (1/J) * F * S * F'
    return σ
end

function viscous_stress(D, η)
    """Viscous stress from rate of deformation"""
    return 2 * η * D
end

function update_deformation_gradient(F_old, L, dt)
    """Update deformation gradient using velocity gradient"""
    # Forward Euler integration (simple example)
    F_new = (I + dt * L) * F_old
    return F_new
end

# Example: Simple shear deformation with viscoelastic response
function finite_viscoelastic_shear(γ_rate, material, t_final, dt)
    """
    Simulate finite viscoelastic response to constant shear rate
    """
    # Initialize
    F = Matrix(1.0I, 3, 3)  # Identity initially
    t_points = 0:dt:t_final
    stress_history = zeros(length(t_points), 3, 3)
    
    # Velocity gradient for simple shear
    L = zeros(3, 3)
    L[1, 2] = γ_rate  # Constant shear rate
    
    for (i, t) in enumerate(t_points)
        if i == 1
            stress_history[i, :, :] = zeros(3, 3)
            continue
        end
        
        # Update deformation gradient
        F = update_deformation_gradient(F, L, dt)
        
        # Rate of deformation tensor
        D = 0.5 * (L + L')
        
        # Hyperelastic stress contribution
        σ_elastic = neo_hookean_stress(F, material.μ, material.K)
        
        # Viscous stress contribution
        σ_viscous = viscous_stress(D, material.η)
        
        # Total stress
        σ_total = σ_elastic + σ_viscous
        stress_history[i, :, :] = σ_total
    end
    
    return t_points, stress_history, F
end

# Material parameters (rubber-like)
material = FiniteViscoelasticMaterial(1e6, 1e6, 1e5, 2e6)

# Simulation parameters
γ_rate = 0.1  # Shear rate (1/s)
t_final = 100.0  # Final time (s)
dt = 0.1  # Time step (s)

# Run simulation
times, stresses, F_final = finite_viscoelastic_shear(γ_rate, material, t_final, dt)

# Extract shear stress component
shear_stress = [stresses[i, 1, 2] for i in 1:length(times)]

# Plot results
plot(times, shear_stress/1e3, xlabel="Time (s)", ylabel="Shear Stress (kPa)",
     label="Finite Viscoelastic Response", lw=2)

5.5 Biological Applications

5.5.1 Soft Tissue Viscoelasticity

Biological soft tissues exhibit pronounced viscoelastic behavior across multiple time scales. The QLV framework proves particularly successful for modeling these materials.

Example 6.5 (Ligament Stress Relaxation). Ligaments show typical stress relaxation curves that can be fit with the QLV model:

σᵉ(ε) = E₀ε + E₁ε² (polynomial elastic response) G(t) = G∞ + (1 - G∞)exp(-t/τ) (normalized relaxation)

Experimental data for human anterior cruciate ligament yields: - E₀ = 120 MPa, E₁ = 450 MPa
- G∞ = 0.15, τ = 240 s

5.5.2 Cellular Mechanics

At the cellular level, viscoelasticity governs processes like cell migration, division, and response to mechanical stimuli. The cytoplasm behaves as a complex viscoelastic fluid with both elastic (cytoskeleton) and viscous (cytosol) components.

using Distributions, StatsBase

# Model for cellular viscoelastic response
struct CellularViscoelastic
    G₀::Float64      # Initial modulus
    G∞::Float64      # Long-term modulus
    τ₁::Float64      # Fast relaxation time
    τ₂::Float64      # Slow relaxation time
    α₁::Float64      # Fast relaxation strength
    α₂::Float64      # Slow relaxation strength
end

function cell_relaxation_modulus(model, t)
    """Bi-exponential relaxation for cellular materials"""
    G∞ + model.α₁ * exp(-t/model.τ₁) + model.α₂ * exp(-t/model.τ₂)
end

function simulate_cell_indentation(model, indentation_depth, contact_radius, t_eval)
    """
    Simulate atomic force microscopy indentation of viscoelastic cell
    Uses Hertzian contact mechanics with viscoelastic correction
    """
    # Hertzian elastic force
    δ = indentation_depth
    a = contact_radius
    
    forces = zeros(length(t_eval))
    
    for (i, t) in enumerate(t_eval)
        if t <= 0
            forces[i] = 0.0
        else
            # Step indentation response
            G_t = cell_relaxation_modulus(model, t)
            # Hertzian contact (simplified)
            E_eff = G_t * (1 - 0.3^2)  # Approximate Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3
            F = (4/3) * E_eff * sqrt(a) * δ^(3/2)
            forces[i] = F
        end
    end
    
    return forces
end

# Typical cellular parameters
cell_model = CellularViscoelastic(
    5000.0,    # G₀ (Pa)
    500.0,     # G∞ (Pa) 
    0.1,       # τ₁ (s) - fast cytoskeletal relaxation
    10.0,      # τ₂ (s) - slow network reorganization
    3000.0,    # α₁ (Pa)
    1500.0     # α₂ (Pa)
)

# Indentation parameters
δ = 1e-6  # 1 μm indentation depth
a = 5e-6  # 5 μm contact radius
t_range = 0:0.01:30

# Simulate response
force_response = simulate_cell_indentation(cell_model, δ, a, t_range)

# Plot cellular viscoelastic response
plot(t_range, force_response*1e9, xlabel="Time (s)", 
     ylabel="Indentation Force (nN)", 
     label="Cellular Viscoelastic Response", lw=2)
title!("AFM Indentation of Viscoelastic Cell")

5.6 Computational Implementation Strategies

5.6.1 Numerical Integration of Hereditary Integrals

The hereditary nature of viscoelastic constitutive laws requires careful numerical treatment. Efficient algorithms must balance accuracy with computational cost, particularly for problems involving long time histories.

5.6.2 Internal Variable Methods

For computational efficiency, hereditary integrals can be replaced with differential equations for internal variables, converting integro-differential problems to purely differential ones.

Theorem 6.4 (Internal Variable Representation). A Prony series relaxation function:

G(t) = G∞ + ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Gᵢ exp(-t/τᵢ)

can be represented using internal variables qᵢ satisfying:

τᵢ dqᵢ/dt + qᵢ = Gᵢ ε

with total stress σ = G∞ε + ∑ᵢqᵢ

This representation proves invaluable for finite element implementations and large-scale computations.

5.7 Exercises

  1. Theoretical Analysis: Prove that for the standard linear solid, the relaxation and creep functions satisfy the reciprocal relationship. Show all steps in the Laplace domain derivation.

  2. Maxwell Model Implementation: Write Julia code to simulate the response of a Maxwell material to a triangular strain input (linear ramp up to peak strain, then linear ramp down to zero). Compare with the analytical solution.

  3. Parameter Identification: Given experimental stress relaxation data for a biological tissue, fit a three-term Prony series model. Use synthetic data generated from known parameters plus random noise, then assess fitting accuracy.

  4. Correspondence Principle: Solve the torsion of a circular shaft made from a Maxwell viscoelastic material subject to a suddenly applied torque. Use the correspondence principle and compare results with direct hereditary integral solution.

  5. Nonlinear Viscoelasticity: Implement the quasi-linear viscoelastic model for an exponential elastic response σᵉ(ε) = A(e^(Bε) - 1). Simulate the response to a multi-step strain history and analyze the nonlinear coupling effects.

  6. Dynamic Mechanical Analysis: Write a function to compute the complex modulus G*(ω) from a Prony series representation. Plot the storage modulus, loss modulus, and loss tangent over a frequency range of 10⁻³ to 10³ Hz.

  7. Finite Element Integration: Develop a numerical scheme for integrating the internal variable representation of viscoelasticity. Use backward Euler time integration and verify stability properties.

  8. Biological Application: Model the stress relaxation of articular cartilage using a biphasic viscoelastic framework. Consider both solid matrix viscoelasticity and fluid flow effects (simplified poroelastic coupling).

  9. Computational Efficiency: Compare the computational cost of direct hereditary integral evaluation versus internal variable methods for a simple boundary value problem. Analyze how the cost scales with the number of time steps and relaxation terms.

  10. Advanced Theory: Derive the evolution equations for finite deformation viscoelasticity using the multiplicative decom \newpage

6 Chapter 7: Biological Tissue Mechanics

Biological tissues exhibit mechanical properties that distinguish them fundamentally from engineered materials. Unlike metals or ceramics, living tissues undergo large deformations, exhibit pronounced anisotropy due to fiber reinforcement, display time-dependent viscoelastic behavior, and possess the remarkable ability to grow, remodel, and actively contract. These unique characteristics necessitate specialized theoretical frameworks that extend the classical theories developed in Chapters 4–6.

The mechanics of biological tissues encompasses multiple scales, from molecular interactions within collagen fibrils to organ-level deformations. At the continuum level, tissues are typically modeled as fiber-reinforced composites with a soft matrix material (elastin, ground substance) reinforced by stiff fibers (collagen, muscle fibers). This microstructural architecture imparts strong directional dependence to the mechanical response.

6.1 7.1 Distinctive Properties of Biological Tissues

6.1.1 7.1.1 Large Deformation Behavior

Biological tissues routinely experience strains exceeding 50%, far beyond the small-strain regime of linear elasticity. Consider arterial tissue during the cardiac cycle: circumferential strains can reach 20-40%, while the tissue remains within its physiological operating range. This necessitates finite strain theory as developed in Chapters 1–3.

The stress-strain response of most soft tissues is characterized by an exponential stiffening behavior. At low strains, the tissue is compliant as crimped collagen fibers straighten. As strain increases, the fibers become taut and the material stiffens dramatically. This J-shaped stress-strain curve provides mechanical protection against overextension.

Definition 7.1 (Toe Region). The toe region of a soft tissue stress-strain curve is the initial low-stiffness regime where crimped fibers uncrimp and straighten without significant load bearing.

6.1.2 7.1.2 Anisotropy and Fiber Reinforcement

Biological tissues derive their mechanical properties from their microstructural organization. Collagen fibers, elastin sheets, and muscle fiber bundles create preferential directions of stiffness and strength. Unlike isotropic materials characterized by two elastic constants, anisotropic tissues require additional material parameters to describe their directional properties.

For a transversely isotropic material with a single preferred direction a₀, the deformation behavior depends on the stretch λ = √(a₀ · Ca₀) in the fiber direction, where C is the right Cauchy-Green tensor from Chapter 1.

Definition 7.2 (Fiber Direction Vector). Let a₀ be a unit vector in the reference configuration indicating the local fiber direction. The fiber stretch is λₐ = √(C : a₀ ⊗ a₀) = √(a₀ · Ca₀).

6.1.3 7.1.3 Incompressibility

Most soft biological tissues contain 60-80% water and exhibit nearly incompressible behavior under physiological loading rates. This constraint significantly reduces the number of independent deformation modes and necessitates special numerical treatment in computational implementations.

The incompressibility constraint J = det(F) = 1 from Chapter 3 applies, requiring modification of constitutive laws to decompose stress into volumetric and isochoric parts.

6.2 7.2 Fiber-Reinforced Hyperelastic Models

6.2.1 7.2.1 Invariant-Based Formulation

For fiber-reinforced tissues, the strain energy function depends on invariants that capture both isotropic matrix behavior and anisotropic fiber contributions. Consider a single family of fibers with direction a₀ in the reference configuration.

The relevant strain invariants are: - I₁ = tr(C) (first isotropic invariant) - I₂ = ½[(tr(C))² - tr(C²)] (second isotropic invariant) - I₄ = a₀ · Ca₀ (fiber stretch invariant) - I₅ = a₀ · C²a₀ (fiber shear invariant)

For incompressible materials, I₃ = det(C) = 1 is automatically satisfied.

Definition 7.3 (Anisotropic Invariants). For a fiber family with direction a₀, the anisotropic strain invariants are I₄ = a₀ · Ca₀ and I₅ = a₀ · C²a₀, representing fiber stretch and fiber shear respectively.

The strain energy density takes the general form: ψ = ψ_iso(I₁, I₂) + ψ_aniso(I₄, I₅)

where ψ_iso captures the isotropic matrix contribution and ψ_aniso represents the fiber contribution.

6.2.2 7.2.2 The Holzapfel-Gasser-Ogden Model

The Holzapfel-Gasser-Ogden (HGO) model has become the gold standard for arterial tissue mechanics. It accounts for fiber dispersion—the distribution of collagen fibers about mean directions rather than perfect alignment.

Definition 7.4 (HGO Model). The Holzapfel-Gasser-Ogden strain energy density is:

ψ = c₁₀(I₁ - 3) + c₂₀(I₂ - 3) + Σᵢ₌₁² k₁ᵢ/k₂ᵢ [exp(k₂ᵢE²ᵢ) - 1]

where Eᵢ = κᵢ(I₁ - 3) + (1 - 3κᵢ)(I₄ᵢ - 1) and κᵢ ∈ [0, 1/3] is the dispersion parameter.

The dispersion parameter κ controls fiber alignment: - κ = 0: perfect fiber alignment (I₄-only dependence) - κ = 1/3: isotropic distribution (I₁-only dependence) - 0 < κ < 1/3: fiber dispersion about mean direction

Example 7.1 (Arterial Wall Under Inflation). Consider a thick-walled arterial segment modeled as a cylindrical tube with inner radius A, outer radius B, and two fiber families at angles ±α to the circumferential direction.

For axisymmetric deformation with radial coordinate R mapping to r = rₐ√(λz), where λz is the axial stretch:

The fiber directions in cylindrical coordinates are: a₀^(1) = cos(α)eΘ + sin(α)eZ a₀^(2) = cos(α)eΘ - sin(α)eZ

The fiber stretches are: I₄₁ = I₄₂ = λΘ² cos²(α) + λz² sin²(α)

where λΘ = r/R is the circumferential stretch.

The radial equilibrium equation becomes: dσᵣ/dr + (σᵣ - σΘ)/r = 0

where the stresses are derived from ∂ψ/∂I₁, ∂ψ/∂I₄₁, etc. through the constitutive relations of Chapter 3.

using Optimization, ForwardDiff

# HGO model parameters for arterial tissue
struct HGOParameters
    c10::Float64    # Matrix parameter
    c20::Float64    # Matrix parameter  
    k11::Float64    # Fiber stiffness family 1
    k21::Float64    # Fiber nonlinearity family 1
    k12::Float64    # Fiber stiffness family 2
    k22::Float64    # Fiber nonlinearity family 2
    kappa1::Float64 # Dispersion parameter family 1
    kappa2::Float64 # Dispersion parameter family 2
    alpha::Float64  # Fiber angle (radians)
end

function hgo_strain_energy(I1, I2, I41, I42, params::HGOParameters)
    """Holzapfel-Gasser-Ogden strain energy density"""
    # Matrix contribution
    psi_matrix = params.c10 * (I1 - 3) + params.c20 * (I2 - 3)
    
    # Fiber family 1
    E1 = params.kappa1 * (I1 - 3) + (1 - 3*params.kappa1) * (I41 - 1)
    psi_fiber1 = if E1 > 0
        params.k11 / params.k21 * (exp(params.k21 * E1^2) - 1)
    else
        0.0  # No compression
    end
    
    # Fiber family 2
    E2 = params.kappa2 * (I1 - 3) + (1 - 3*params.kappa2) * (I42 - 1)
    psi_fiber2 = if E2 > 0
        params.k12 / params.k22 * (exp(params.k22 * E2^2) - 1)
    else
        0.0  # No compression
    end
    
    return psi_matrix + psi_fiber1 + psi_fiber2
end

function arterial_inflation_residual(lambda_theta, lambda_z, params, pressure)
    """Residual for arterial inflation equilibrium"""
    # Fiber stretches for ±α configuration
    cos_alpha, sin_alpha = cos(params.alpha), sin(params.alpha)
    I41 = I42 = lambda_theta^2 * cos_alpha^2 + lambda_z^2 * sin_alpha^2
    
    # Strain invariants
    lambda_r = 1.0 / (lambda_theta * lambda_z)  # Incompressibility
    I1 = lambda_r^2 + lambda_theta^2 + lambda_z^2
    I2 = lambda_r^2 * lambda_theta^2 + lambda_theta^2 * lambda_z^2 + lambda_z^2 * lambda_r^2
    
    # Stress computation via automatic differentiation
    psi = I -> hgo_strain_energy(I[1], I[2], I[3], I[4], params)
    grad_psi = ForwardDiff.gradient(psi, [I1, I2, I41, I42])
    
    # Cauchy stress components (simplified for illustration)
    # Full implementation requires proper tensor operations
    sigma_theta_minus_sigma_r = 2 * lambda_theta^2 * grad_psi[1] + 
                               2 * lambda_theta^2 * grad_psi[2] * (lambda_r^2 + lambda_z^2) +
                               4 * lambda_theta^2 * cos_alpha^2 * grad_psi[3]
    
    # Equilibrium: integrate radial stress from inner to outer radius
    # This is simplified - full solution requires numerical integration
    return sigma_theta_minus_sigma_r - pressure
end

# Example: solve for circumferential stretch under pressure
params = HGOParameters(2.3e3, 0.0, 2.3e3, 0.893, 4.289e2, 0.216, 0.226, 0.0012, π/4)
pressure = 13.33e3  # 100 mmHg in Pa
lambda_z = 1.1      # 10% axial stretch

# Solve for circumferential stretch
result = optimize(λθ -> arterial_inflation_residual(λθ[1], lambda_z, params, pressure)^2, 
                  [1.2], Newton())
lambda_theta_eq = result.minimizer[1]

println("Equilibrium circumferential stretch: $(lambda_theta_eq)")
println("Circumferential strain: $((lambda_theta_eq - 1) * 100)%")

6.2.3 7.2.3 Fiber Dispersion Models

Real biological tissues do not have perfectly aligned fibers. Collagen fibers exhibit statistical dispersion about mean directions, characterized by orientation distribution functions (ODFs). The simplest approach uses the dispersion parameter κ in the HGO model, but more sophisticated models employ von Mises or other distributions.

Definition 7.5 (Von Mises Distribution). A von Mises distribution on the unit sphere with mean direction a₀ and concentration parameter b has probability density:

ρ(n) = (4π sinh(b))⁻¹ exp(b(a₀ · n))

For large b, fibers are highly aligned; for small b, fibers are nearly isotropic.

6.3 7.3 Viscoelasticity in Biological Tissues

Most biological tissues exhibit time-dependent behavior—creep under constant stress and stress relaxation under constant strain. This viscoelasticity arises from multiple mechanisms: fluid flow through the porous matrix, protein unfolding and refolding, and cross-link breaking and reformation.

6.3.1 7.3.1 Quasilinear Viscoelasticity

Fung’s quasilinear viscoelasticity (QLV) theory provides a framework for nonlinear, time-dependent behavior while maintaining mathematical tractability. The theory assumes that the stress response factorizes into elastic and time-dependent parts.

Definition 7.6 (Quasilinear Viscoelasticity). In Fung’s QLV theory, the stress at time t is:

σ(t) = G(t)σᵉ(E(t)) + ∫₀ᵗ ∂G(t-s)/∂s σᵉ(E(s))ds

where σᵉ(E) is the elastic response, E(t) is the strain history, and G(t) is the reduced relaxation function.

The reduced relaxation function G(t) is independent of strain magnitude, typically modeled as:

G(t) = 1 - Σᵢ Aᵢ(1 - e⁻ᵗ/τᵢ)

with relaxation amplitudes Aᵢ and time constants τᵢ.

Example 7.2 (Stress Relaxation Test). Consider a uniaxial stress relaxation experiment where strain is applied instantaneously at t = 0 and held constant. For a Prony series relaxation function:

σ(t) = E₀[1 - Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ Aᵢ(1 - e⁻ᵗ/τᵢ)]ε₀

where E₀ is the instantaneous modulus and ε₀ is the applied strain.

using DifferentialEquations, Plots

# Prony series viscoelastic model
struct PronyModel
    E0::Float64      # Instantaneous modulus
    A::Vector{Float64}  # Relaxation amplitudes  
    tau::Vector{Float64}  # Relaxation times
end

function stress_relaxation(model::PronyModel, strain0::Float64, t_max::Float64)
    """Compute stress relaxation response"""
    t = range(0, t_max, length=1000)
    
    # Prony series stress relaxation
    stress = zeros(length(t))
    for (i, time) in enumerate(t)
        if time == 0
            stress[i] = model.E0 * strain0
        else
            relaxation_sum = sum(model.A[j] * (1 - exp(-time/model.tau[j])) 
                               for j in 1:length(model.A))
            stress[i] = model.E0 * (1 - relaxation_sum) * strain0
        end
    end
    
    return t, stress
end

# Example: arterial tissue viscoelastic parameters
artery_model = PronyModel(1.5e6, [0.4, 0.3, 0.2], [1.0, 10.0, 100.0])

# Stress relaxation under 10% strain
t, sigma = stress_relaxation(artery_model, 0.1, 200.0)

# [FIGURE: Stress relaxation curve showing exponential decay from initial stress 
#  to equilibrium stress over 200 seconds, with characteristic time constants]

# Compute relaxation percentages
initial_stress = sigma[1]
final_stress = sigma[end]
relaxation_percent = (initial_stress - final_stress) / initial_stress * 100

println("Initial stress: $(initial_stress/1000) kPa")
println("Final stress: $(final_stress/1000) kPa") 
println("Relaxation: $(relaxation_percent)%")

6.3.2 7.3.2 Finite Strain Viscoelasticity

For large deformation viscoelasticity, several approaches extend linear theory. The multiplicative decomposition F = FᵉFⁱ treats viscoelastic flow as inelastic deformation, while integral formulations generalize the QLV approach to finite strains.

In the multiplicative approach, the deformation gradient splits into elastic and inelastic parts. Evolution equations for Fⁱ depend on stress and internal variables representing the microstructural state.

6.4 7.4 Active Tissue Mechanics

Muscle tissues possess the unique ability to generate active stress through actin-myosin interactions. This active stress generation fundamentally alters the mechanical response and requires specialized modeling approaches.

6.4.1 7.4.1 Active Stress vs. Active Strain

Two primary approaches model muscle activity: active stress and active strain formulations.

Definition 7.7 (Active Stress). In the active stress approach, the total Cauchy stress decomposes as:

σ = σᵖᵃˢˢⁱᵛᵉ + σᵃᶜᵗⁱᵛᵉ

where σᵖᵃˢˢⁱᵛᵉ follows from passive hyperelastic constitutive relations and σᵃᶜᵗⁱᵛᵉ represents muscle contraction.

Definition 7.8 (Active Strain). In the active strain approach, the deformation gradient multiplicatively decomposes as:

F = FᵉFᵃ

where Fᵉ is the elastic deformation and Fᵃ represents active contraction.

The active stress approach is simpler to implement but may violate thermodynamic principles for large contractions. The active strain approach maintains thermodynamic consistency but requires additional evolution equations for Fᵃ.

6.4.2 7.4.2 Muscle Fiber Activation

Active stress generation depends on muscle fiber activation, calcium concentration, and fiber stretch. The Hill muscle model captures these dependencies:

σᵃᶜᵗⁱᵛᵉ = γ(t)f(λ)g(λ̇)n₀ ⊗ n₀

where: - γ(t) is the neural activation (0 ≤ γ ≤ 1) - f(λ) is the force-length relation
- g(λ̇) is the force-velocity relation - n₀ is the muscle fiber direction

Example 7.3 (Cardiac Contraction Cycle). Consider a simplified left ventricle model during the cardiac cycle. The myocardium contains muscle fibers at varying angles through the wall thickness.

using Catalyst, DifferentialEquations

# Simplified cardiac activation model
@parameters t
@variables Ca(t) γ(t)  # Calcium concentration and activation

# Calcium transient model (simplified)
@reaction_network CardiacActivation begin
    k_ca, 0 --> Ca
    k_rel, Ca --> 0  
end k_ca k_rel

# Force-length relationship (Gordon et al.)
function force_length_relation(lambda::Float64)
    """Normalized active force vs. fiber stretch"""
    lambda_opt = 1.0  # Optimal stretch
    if lambda < 0.6
        return 0.0
    elseif lambda < lambda_opt
        return (lambda - 0.6) / (lambda_opt - 0.6)
    elseif lambda < 1.4
        return 1.0 - 0.5 * (lambda - lambda_opt)^2
    else
        return max(0.0, 1.0 - 2.0 * (lambda - lambda_opt))
    end
end

# Force-velocity relationship (Hill equation)
function force_velocity_relation(lambda_dot::Float64, v_max::Float64=1.0)
    """Normalized active force vs. contraction velocity"""
    if lambda_dot <= 0  # Concentric contraction
        return (v_max + lambda_dot) / (v_max - lambda_dot/2)
    else  # Eccentric contraction  
        return min(1.5, 1.0 + 0.3 * lambda_dot / v_max)
    end
end

# Active stress computation
function active_stress_fiber(activation::Float64, lambda::Float64, 
                           lambda_dot::Float64, T_max::Float64=50e3)
    """Active stress along fiber direction (Pa)"""
    f_l = force_length_relation(lambda)
    f_v = force_velocity_relation(lambda_dot)
    return activation * T_max * f_l * f_v
end

# Example cardiac cycle simulation
t_span = (0.0, 1.0)  # One cardiac cycle (1 second)
t_eval = range(0, 1, length=100)

# Simplified activation function (calcium transient)
activation(t) = if t < 0.3
    0.8 * sin(π * t / 0.3)^2
else
    0.8 * exp(-(t - 0.3) / 0.2) * sin(π * t / 0.3)^2
end

# Fiber stretch from ventricular filling/ejection
fiber_stretch(t) = if t < 0.4  # Filling
    1.0 + 0.2 * sin(π * t / 0.4)
elseif t < 0.8  # Ejection
    1.2 - 0.3 * sin(π * (t - 0.4) / 0.4)
else  # Relaxation
    0.9 + 0.1 * sin(π * (t - 0.8) / 0.2)
end

# Compute active stress throughout cycle
lambda_vals = [fiber_stretch(t) for t in t_eval]
lambda_dot_vals = ForwardDiff.derivative.(fiber_stretch, t_eval)
gamma_vals = [activation(t) for t in t_eval]

active_stress_vals = [active_stress_fiber(gamma_vals[i], lambda_vals[i], 
                                        lambda_dot_vals[i]) for i in 1:length(t_eval)]

println("Peak active stress: $(maximum(active_stress_vals)/1000) kPa")
println("Occurs at time: $(t_eval[argmax(active_stress_vals)]) s")

# [FIGURE: Three-panel plot showing (top) calcium activation vs time, 
#  (middle) fiber stretch vs time, (bottom) active stress vs time during 
#  cardiac cycle, highlighting systolic peak]

6.5 7.5 Residual Stress and Opening Angle Experiments

Living tissues exist in a state of residual stress even in the absence of external loads. This residual stress arises from differential growth, remodeling processes, and the need to homogenize stress distributions under physiological loading.

6.5.1 7.5.1 Opening Angle Experiments

The classical demonstration of residual stress involves cutting a ring of arterial tissue radially. The cut ring springs open to a characteristic opening angle, revealing the stored residual stress.

Definition 7.9 (Opening Angle). The opening angle Φ₀ is the angle subtended by an arterial ring after a radial cut relieves circumferential residual stress. It relates to the stress-free inner radius aₛf and outer radius bₛf through geometric constraints.

For a thick-walled arterial segment, the relationship between the loaded configuration (inner radius A, outer radius B) and the stress-free configuration (inner radius aₛf, outer radius bₛf, opening angle Φ₀) involves solving the equilibrium equations with residual stress boundary conditions.

The residual stress distribution typically follows:

σᵣᵣʳᵉˢ(R) = 0 (traction-free surfaces) σ_θθʳᵉˢ(R) = ∫ᴬᴿ [2ψ₁ + 4ψ₂(I₁ - λ²)] dR’/R’

where ψ₁ and ψ₂ are derivatives of the strain energy with respect to I₁ and I₂.

6.5.2 7.5.2 Growth and Residual Stress Generation

Residual stresses develop through differential growth and remodeling. As tissues adapt to altered mechanical environments, cells modulate their production of extracellular matrix proteins, leading to local changes in reference configuration.

Example 7.4 (Arterial Remodeling Under Hypertension). Consider an artery subject to elevated pressure over time. The tissue responds by thickening the wall and adjusting fiber orientations to restore stress homeostasis.

using ForwardDiff, Optimization

# Arterial geometry and residual stress model
struct ArterialGeometry
    A::Float64      # Inner radius (loaded)
    B::Float64      # Outer radius (loaded) 
    A_sf::Float64   # Inner radius (stress-free)
    B_sf::Float64   # Outer radius (stress-free)
    phi0::Float64   # Opening angle (radians)
end

function stress_free_to_loaded_mapping(R_sf::Float64, geom::ArterialGeometry)
    """Map stress-free radius to loaded configuration"""
    # Conservation of volume between configurations
    alpha = geom.phi0 / (2π)  # Fraction of full circle in stress-free state
    
    # Volume conservation: π(B² - A²) = α⋅π(B_sf² - A_sf²)  
    # Assumes radial mapping R = R(R_sf)
    
    if R_sf ≤ geom.A_sf
        return geom.A
    elseif R_sf ≥ geom.B_sf
        return geom.B
    else
        # Linear interpolation (simplified)
        xi = (R_sf - geom.A_sf) / (geom.B_sf - geom.A_sf)
        return geom.A + xi * (geom.B - geom.A)
    end
end

function compute_opening_angle(inner_stress::Float64, outer_stress::Float64, 
                              thickness::Float64, E_theta::Float64)
    """Estimate opening angle from residual stress distribution"""
    # Simplified relationship based on stress moment balance
    stress_moment = (outer_stress - inner_stress) * thickness^2 / 6
    return abs(stress_moment) / (E_theta * thickness)  # Normalized
end

# Example: normal vs. hypertensive artery
normal_artery = ArterialGeometry(3.0e-3, 4.5e-3, 2.8e-3, 4.8e-3, π/6)
hypertensive_artery = ArterialGeometry(3.2e-3, 5.2e-3, 2.9e-3, 5.5e-3, π/4)

println("Normal artery opening angle: $(normal_artery.phi0 * 180/π)°")
println("Hypertensive artery opening angle: $(hypertensive_artery.phi0 * 180/π)°")

# Residual stress profiles (simplified calculation)
function residual_stress_profile(geom::ArterialGeometry, n_points::Int=50)
    """Compute residual circumferential stress distribution"""
    R_points = range(geom.A, geom.B, length=n_points)
    sigma_theta_res = zeros(n_points)
    
    for (i, R) in enumerate(R_points)
        # Simplified: residual stress from opening angle
        # Real calculation requires solving equilibrium equations
        normalized_radius = (R - geom.A) / (geom.B - geom.A)
        sigma_theta_res[i] = geom.phi0 * 50e3 * (0.5 - normalized_radius)
    end
    
    return R_points, sigma_theta_res
end

R_norm, sigma_norm = residual_stress_profile(normal_artery)
R_hyper, sigma_hyper = residual_stress_profile(hypertensive_artery)

# [FIGURE: Comparison of residual stress profiles showing increased residual 
#  stress magnitude in hypertensive artery, with tension on inner wall and 
#  compression on outer wall]

println("Peak residual stress (normal): $(maximum(abs.(sigma_norm))/1000) kPa")
println("Peak residual stress (hypertensive): $(maximum(abs.(sigma_hyper))/1000) kPa")

6.6 7.6 Mixture Theory for Soft Tissues

Biological soft tissues are inherently multiphase materials consisting of solid matrix (collagen, elastin) and interstitial fluid. Mixture theory provides the framework for analyzing the mechanical behavior of such composite materials.

6.6.1 7.6.1 Biphasic Theory

The simplest mixture model treats tissue as a biphasic medium with solid and fluid phases. Each phase has its own motion, and interactions between phases generate drag forces and chemical potential gradients.

Definition 7.10 (Biphasic Mixture). A biphasic mixture consists of: - Solid phase (s): incompressible elastic solid with volume fraction φˢ - Fluid phase (f): incompressible viscous fluid with volume fraction φᶠ = 1 - φˢ - Constraint: φˢ + φᶠ = 1 (saturation condition)

The mixture velocity is the volume-averaged velocity: v = φˢvˢ + φᶠvᶠ

The relative fluid velocity represents fluid flow through the porous solid: w = φᶠ(vᶠ - vˢ)

6.6.2 7.6.2 Balance Equations for Mixtures

Each phase satisfies its own mass and momentum balance equations, coupled through interaction terms.
